parentQuestion: Motion sickness seems to result from mismatch between visual and vestibular inputs.

scaffolds.q: The vestibular system (inner ear) detects head movement and position.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Semicircular canals detect rotation; maculae detect linear motion and gravity.

scaffolds.q: Your eyes also provide information about motion through visual cues.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Moving visual scenes tell your brain you're moving through space.

scaffolds.q: Normally, visual and vestibular systems agree about whether you're moving.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! When walking, eyes see movement, vestibular system detects motion — they match.

scaffolds.q: Reading in a car: Vestibular says 'moving,' but eyes focused on book say 'stationary.'
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This mismatch confuses the brain — sensory conflict.

scaffolds.q: Motion sickness includes nausea, sweating, and malaise.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The brainstem responds to sensory conflict with autonomic symptoms.

scaffolds.q: Looking at the horizon in a car helps because it synchronizes visual and vestibular inputs.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Seeing external motion matches what the vestibular system detects.

scaffolds.q: The evolutionary theory: Sensory mismatch historically suggested poisoning, triggering vomiting.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Toxins can cause sensory disturbances. Vomiting would expel the poison.

scaffolds.q: VR sickness occurs because eyes see motion but vestibular system detects stillness.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! 'Cybersickness' is the opposite mismatch of traditional motion sickness.

scaffolds.q: Motion sickness results from conflict between visual and vestibular sensory inputs.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! When eyes and inner ear disagree about motion, the brain triggers nausea.
